mnemonics.title: Double Diamond Phases
mnemonics.content: The 4 Ds: Discover (research), Define (insights), Develop (solutions), Deliver (product). Think of it as diamond shape: diverge to explore, converge to decide, diverge to create, converge to deliver.
AGILE Design: A - Always, G - Get, I - Input, L - Like, E - Everyone involved. In Agile, design happens continuously with all team members collaborating in iterations.
DESIGN Handoff Checklist: S - Specifications, P - Prototypes, E - Assets, E - Examples, D - Details (spacing, colors), S - States (hover, active). Think SPEEDS for smooth handoff.

acronyms.usage: The overall experience of a user when interacting with a product, including usability, accessibility, and satisfaction.
The visual and interactive elements of a product that users interact with, including buttons, menus, and layouts.
The simplest version of a product that can be released to validate ideas with real users before adding more features.
A methodology emphasizing rapid prototyping and iterative development with user feedback.
The process of designing interfaces with focus on styling and interactivity.
An approach where user needs and preferences guide all design decisions throughout development.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two triangles tip-to-tip: first triangle (wide top, pointing down) represents exploring divergent ideas then converging on definition; second triangle (pointing up then widening) represents developing solutions then delivering final product.
Picture a loop where design and development circles overlap completely, with each sprint containing both design and build phases - unlike waterfall where they are sequential boxes.
Think of a pyramid: Top = Brand values, Middle = Design language (tokens, typography), Base = Component library (buttons, forms) - each level informs the one below.
A ladder with 4 rungs: 1-Paper sketches, 2-Wireframes (gray boxes), 3-High-fidelity mockups (colors), 4-Interactive prototype (clicks work). Climb as you validate more.

analogies.analogy: Like a chef passing a recipe to a cook - the recipe must include ingredients (assets), steps (specifications), and plating instructions (visual details) for the dish to turn out right.
Like buying furniture that needs assembly - taking shortcuts (skipping assembly instructions) saves time now but creates mess and rework later when you need to fix it properly.
Like a LEGO set - standardized pieces (components) with instructions (guidelines) that can be combined in endless ways to build consistent structures.
Like a test drive before buying a car - you don't just read the manual, you actually use it to discover if it meets your needs and feels right.
Like a sports team with specialized players - designers are like coaches planning strategy, developers are like players executing, and they must communicate constantly to win.
